senses.definition: A type of igneous rock similar to basalt, rich in certain minerals, used mainly as a technical geological term.
examples: The cliff face is composed largely of anamesite, giving it a dark, dense appearance.
Under the microscope, the anamesite sample revealed a fine-grained crystalline structure.

senses.definition: Describing a plant vein or nerve that runs in the opposite direction to the main axis, used in specialized botanical description.
examples: The leaf shows several anametadromous veins branching away from the midrib.
In this species, anametadromous venation helps distinguish it from related plants.

senses.definition: A genus of climbing plants, especially Anamirta cocculus, native to South and Southeast Asia and known as the source of the poisonous compound picrotoxin.
examples: Anamirta cocculus has long been used in traditional medicine, though it is highly toxic.
Researchers isolated picrotoxin from the dried fruit of Anamirta.

senses.definition: A crystalline chemical compound obtained from the plant Anamirta cocculus, also known as cocculin, studied in chemistry and pharmacology.
examples: Anamirtin was first identified as a component of the toxic mixture obtained from Anamirta cocculus seeds.
The laboratory succeeded in purifying anamirtin into white crystalline needles.

senses.definition: An older spelling or variant of Annamite, referring to a person from the former region of Annam in Vietnam; now mainly of historical interest.
examples: French colonial records often described local workers simply as Anamites.
The novel features an Anamite guide who leads the expedition through the mountains.

senses.definition: Relating to Annam or its people; a historical or dated ethnographic term.
examples: The text offers a detailed account of Anamite customs and rituals.
He specialized in Anamite dialects during his linguistic studies.

senses.definition: Not containing ammonium, or lacking ammonium compounds, used in specialized chemical or biological description.
examples: The researchers compared ammonid and anammonid growth media in their experiment.
Anammonid conditions prevented the usual nitrogen cycle from taking place.

senses.definition: A compound or salt that does not contain ammonium, used in technical chemical classification.
examples: The sample was classified as an anammonide based on its lack of ammonium ions.
Several inorganic anammonides were synthesized for comparison with their ammonium analogues.

senses.definition: Plural of anamnesis: detailed histories or recollections, especially medical histories taken from different patients.
examples: The physician reviewed the anamneses before beginning the ward round.
Accurate anamneses improve the quality of diagnosis and treatment planning.

senses.definition: A patient’s past medical history, obtained by asking questions during a clinical interview.
examples: Taking a careful anamnesis is often the most important step in making a diagnosis.
Her anamnesis revealed several previous operations and allergies.

senses.definition: The act of remembering past events; a formal or literary term for recollection.
examples: The memoir is a moving anamnesis of his years in exile.
In philosophy, anamnesis can refer to the soul’s recollection of eternal truths.

senses.definition: A part of the Christian Eucharistic prayer that recalls the Passion, Resurrection, and Ascension of Christ.
examples: In the Eucharistic liturgy, the priest introduces the anamnesis after the words of institution.
The church’s catechism explains the meaning of anamnesis in the Mass.

senses.definition: Relating to anamnesis or to a person’s medical history.
examples: The doctor relied heavily on anamnestic data when forming the diagnosis.
Anamnestic information suggested a strong family tendency to heart disease.

senses.definition: Describing an immune response that is faster and stronger when the body meets an antigen for a second time.
examples: Vaccination produces an anamnestic response if the person later encounters the virus again.
The booster shot triggered a strong anamnestic reaction in the patient’s immune system.

senses.definition: In a way that relates to or is based on a person’s medical history.
examples: The condition was anamnestically associated with previous head trauma.
Anamnestically, the symptoms had been present for more than five years.

senses.definition: A historical zoological term for a group of animals that do not develop an amnion (a protective membrane around the embryo), such as fish and amphibians.
examples: Older textbooks sometimes classified vertebrates into Anamnia and Amniota.
In the Anamnia, the embryo develops directly in the surrounding water.

senses.definition: Animals that lack an amnion during embryonic development; a technical zoological group including fish and amphibians.
examples: The anamniata are characterized by their eggs developing in water without an amniotic sac.
In contrast to mammals, anamniata lay eggs that must remain in moist environments.

senses.definition: A zoological group whose embryos develop without an amnion; another form of the term anamniota, now rarely used.
examples: Some nineteenth-century biologists used the term Anamnionata for fishes and amphibians.
Anamnionata were contrasted with Amnionata, which possess an amniotic membrane.

senses.definition: Not having an amnion during embryonic development.
examples: Fish and amphibians are anamniotic vertebrates.
Anamniotic eggs cannot survive on land without a water source.

senses.definition: A major group of vertebrate animals, including fishes and amphibians, whose embryos do not develop an amnion.
examples: In some classifications, living vertebrates are divided into Anamniota and Amniota.
The Anamniota depend on aquatic environments for reproduction.

senses.definition: A vertebrate animal such as a fish or amphibian whose embryo develops without an amnion.
examples: The frog is an example of an anamniote.
Anamniotes usually lay their eggs in water or very moist habitats.

senses.definition: Plural of anamniote: vertebrate animals, such as fishes and amphibians, whose embryos lack an amnion.
examples: Anamniotes represent the more primitive condition among vertebrates.
Unlike amniotes, anamniotes must return to water to reproduce.

senses.definition: Describing an image or projection that looks distorted from a normal angle but appears correct from a special viewpoint or with special lenses.
examples: The artist painted an anamorphic skull that is visible only from the side.
Filmmakers often use anamorphic lenses to capture a wide image on standard film.

senses.definition: In biology, describing a stage or form of an organism that differs from the usual or adult form.
examples: The fungus has an anamorphic stage in which it reproduces asexually.
Anamorphic forms can make identification of some species difficult.

senses.definition: The process or result of producing an anamorphic image that looks right only from a special viewpoint.
examples: Anamorphism has been used in street art to create dramatic optical illusions.
In optics, anamorphism explains why the image appears stretched until viewed with the correct lens.

senses.definition: Plural of anamorphism: different instances of optical or artistic distortion that appear normal from a specific viewpoint.
examples: The exhibition showcases several anamorphisms along the main corridor.
Street artists around the world experiment with pavement anamorphisms.

senses.definition: A device, often a specially shaped mirror or lens, used to view a distorted picture so that it appears normal.
examples: Visitors used an anamorphoscope to see the hidden image on the gallery floor.
The cylindrical anamorphoscope corrected the stretched drawing into a clear portrait.

senses.definition: Plural of anamorphoscope: devices used to make distorted pictures appear normal when viewed through them.
examples: The museum displays several anamorphoscopes from the seventeenth century.
Children enjoyed looking through the anamorphoscopes to discover hidden images.

senses.definition: To distort an image so that it looks correct only when seen from a special viewpoint or through a special device.
examples: The artist chose to anamorphose the landscape so it appears normal only in a mirror cylinder.
Designers anamorphose company logos for use in projection mapping shows.

senses.definition: Plural of anamorphosis: distorted images or projections that appear correct only from a particular angle or with a special device.
examples: The pavement was decorated with colourful anamorphoses that looked realistic from the entrance.
She published a book on the history of optical anamorphoses in European art.

senses.definition: A distorted picture or projection that looks correct only when viewed from a certain angle or reflected in a special mirror.
examples: The famous painting hides a skull in anamorphosis at the bottom of the canvas.
Street artists often use anamorphosis to make flat drawings appear three-dimensional.

senses.definition: A type of optical attachment, especially for cameras or projectors, that creates or corrects an anamorphic image.
examples: The projector uses an anamorphote to stretch the image onto a wide screen.
By adding an anamorphote, the filmmaker achieved a cinematic widescreen effect.

senses.definition: Having a shape that is distorted or different from normal, especially in an optical or artistic sense; used rarely.
examples: The mural presents an anamorphous figure that only resolves when seen from the doorway.
He studied the anamorphous shadows cast by the sculptural installation.

senses.definition: An old or dialect form meaning ‘what?’ or ‘pardon?’, used when asking someone to repeat what they said.
examples: “Anan?” he cried, cupping his ear to hear better.
The old sailor turned and said, “Anan? Speak up, lad.”

senses.definition: Another word for pineapple, used mainly in some languages and older English texts.
examples: The plantation mainly grows anana for export as fresh fruit.
In some markets, pineapple is still labeled as anana on the crates.

senses.definition: A variant or mis-spelled form of Ananaples, probably a proper name, used only in very limited or historical contexts.
examples: The manuscript refers to a minor noble named Ananaplas in passing.
Very little is known about Ananaplas beyond a few brief mentions.

senses.definition: A rare proper name, possibly of a person or place, appearing only in a few specialized or historical sources.
examples: The legend tells of King Ananaples, ruler of a forgotten island.
Ananaples appears only once in the surviving chronicles.

senses.definition: The pineapple fruit; a sweet tropical fruit with thick skin and many spiky leaves.
examples: She added fresh ananas to the fruit salad for extra sweetness.
Ananas grows well in warm, tropical climates.

senses.definition: Plural of ananas: several pineapple fruits.
examples: Crates of ananases were stacked at the back of the market.
They grow ananases and bananas on their small farm.

senses.definition: A Sanskrit word meaning deep happiness or bliss, used especially in spiritual or philosophical writing.
examples: In many Indian traditions, true ananda is said to arise from inner peace.
Meditation, they believe, leads to ananda beyond everyday pleasure.

senses.definition: A chemical made naturally in the brain that acts as a messenger in the endocannabinoid system and can produce feelings of pleasure or well-being.
examples: Anandamide was the first endocannabinoid neurotransmitter to be discovered in humans.
Researchers think that anandamide helps regulate mood, memory, and pain.

senses.definition: Plural of anandamide: molecules of this endocannabinoid found in the body or studied in experiments.
examples: Elevated anandamides may contribute to reduced anxiety in some patients.
Scientists measured how quickly anandamides break down in the brain.

senses.definition: Plural of ananda: states or experiences of deep happiness or spiritual joy.
examples: The text describes the different anandas reached on the path to enlightenment.
They sang about the anandas that follow selfless service.

senses.definition: Having separate male parts, or having an unusual arrangement of male reproductive organs, used in old or rare botanical descriptions.
examples: The flower was once described as anandrarious because of its irregular stamens.
Anandrarious structures are mentioned only in early botanical literature.

senses.definition: The state of having no male sexual organs, used mainly in old scientific or medical writing.
examples: Some early authors used the term anandria for plants lacking visible stamens.
Anandria in animals was considered a serious developmental defect.

senses.definition: Lacking male sexual organs; used in specialized descriptions of plants or animals.
examples: The specimen was anandrious, with no trace of testes.
Anandrious flowers bear only female reproductive parts.

senses.definition: Having no stamens or male organs; used mainly in botanical classification.
examples: Anandrous flowers produce fruit without visible male parts.
The plant is described as anandrous in older taxonomic keys.

senses.definition: Describing something that appears newly formed or recently developed in a series, used in rare technical or philosophical writing.
examples: The author refers to an ananepionic stage in the evolution of legal systems.
These ananepionic forms mark a fresh beginning in the artwork.

senses.definition: Without blood vessels, or resembling tissue that does not contain blood vessels, used in specialized medical or biological description.
examples: Cartilage is essentially anangioid, receiving nutrients by diffusion.
Anangioid tissues present special challenges for healing.

senses.definition: Not having clear angles or corners; lacking an angular shape.
examples: The creature has an anangular body that seems almost perfectly round.
Designers chose ananagular forms to create a softer interior space.

senses.definition: A man in the New Testament who lied about money given to the early Christian church and died suddenly, used as a biblical reference.
examples: The sermon retold the story of Ananias and his wife Sapphira.
He compared the corrupt official to Ananias, who lied to the apostles.

senses.definition: A person who lies, especially in a bold or shameless way, used humorously or critically.
examples: Anyone who believes that Ananias is telling the truth is naïve.
The newspapers branded him an Ananias after the scandal.

senses.definition: An old term sometimes used for masturbation or for sexual practices believed to ‘waste seed’, based on the biblical story of Onan.
examples: Nineteenth-century doctors wrote moralistic tracts warning against ananism.
The concept of ananism reflects outdated medical and religious beliefs.

senses.definition: A rare word likely used as a variant of Annamite for a person from Annam (central Vietnam), now only of historical interest.
examples: The old travel journal mentions an Ananite porter who led the way.
An Ananite trader is described in the report as skilled and quiet.

senses.definition: Relating to or showing obsessive or compulsive behavior, especially in psychiatric description.
examples: He was diagnosed with an anankastic personality disorder characterized by extreme perfectionism.
An anankastic need for order can interfere with everyday life.
